selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content: You must return JSON only. ALL fields in the schema must be present, even if null or empty.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: You know, at my age, I've never been much for these social media contraptions, and frankly, that might be one of the smartest decisions I've made. But I've watched my neighbors and some of the folks down at the community center get absolutely bamboozled by false stories and doctored photographs spreading across Facebook and the like. It's troubling, really, because people aren't equipped to sort truth from fiction anymore. Back in my day, if you read something in the newspaper or heard it on the radio, there was at least some editorial oversight. Now anyone can post anything, and it spreads like wildfire before anyone bothers to check if it's real. The way I see it, if you're going to use these platforms, you need to approach them with the same skepticism you'd use when a stranger knocks on your door selling something too good to be true. First, verify before you share. I've seen my friend Margaret pass along stories about miracle cures and political conspiracies without ever asking herself whether the source was credible. That's dangerous. Second, understand that photographs can be manipulated nowadays in ways that would've seemed like magic when I was younger. A picture used to be worth a thousand words of truth, but not anymore. Third, and this is where my stubbornness comes in handy, don't be afraid to disagree with what you read. Just because something appears on your screen doesn't make it gospel. Check multiple sources, look for corroboration from established news organizations, and ask yourself who benefits from spreading this particular story. My faith teaches me to seek truth and wisdom, and that applies just as much to the digital world as the physical one. The responsibility ultimately falls on the user to be discerning and thoughtful.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 89-year-old retired man with no social media presence, moderate openness to experience, low agreeableness, and high extraversion. His lack of direct social media engagement positions him as an outside observer who can offer perspective on the phenomenon. His curiosity drives his interest in understanding the topic despite not participating. His low agreeableness suggests he'd be skeptical and direct rather than deferential. His moderate conscientiousness means he'd focus on practical rather than exhaustive approaches. His Christian faith and life experience inform his emphasis on truth-seeking and moral responsibility. His extraversion suggests he'd draw on community observations and interpersonal examples rather than abstract reasoning.
response_parsed.tone: Thoughtful, observational, slightly skeptical, grandfatherly yet firm, drawing on life experience and community observations rather than technical expertise. Direct and honest without being harsh, with occasional references to how things were different in earlier decades.
